data.title: The Gabelli Global Content & Connectivity Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Dear Shareholders

For the quarter ended March 31, 2025, the net asset value per Class I Share of The Gabelli Global Content & Connectivity Fund (MUTF:GABTX) increased by 3.7%, outperforming the MSCI AC World Communication Services Index, which recorded a loss of 2.4%.

Global equities declined in 1Q'25, with the MSCI AC World Index down by 1.2%, as movements of U.S. and international stocks diverged. During 1Q, equity market weakness in the U.S. (with S&P 500 down 4.3% on concerns about the new administration's tariff policies and potential economic slowdown) more than offset strength in the rest of the world (MSCI ACWI ex-USA Index was up 5.2%, led by 10.5% appreciation in European stocks on investor rotation into value equities and commitment to increased military and infrastructure spending in Germany). Energy (+9.4%) and Utilities (+6.7%) were the strongest performing sectors in the quarter, while Communication Services (-2.4%) finished in the bottom quartile.

PERFORMANCE DISCUSSION

Leading the list of positive contributors to Fund performance in 1Q'25 was T-Mobile US (TMUS, 9.4% of net assets as of March 31, 2025, +21.3% in 1Q), as the company reported better than expected 4Q'24 results and provided stronger than anticipated 2025 guidance for postpaid net adds and free cash flow. T-Mobile's parent company, Deutsche Telekom (OTCQX:DTEGY, 6.1%, +23.6%) benefited from strong TMUS performance, implementation of price increases in German mobile and broadband, and, perhaps, contract extension by its CEO to 2028 (from 2026). Prosus (OTCPK:PROSY, 5.5%, +15.8%) was up largely due to an 18% gain (in U.S. dollar terms) in shares of Tencent (its largest portfolio position), which is seen as a beneficiary of DeepSeek's Al models through their integration into Tencent's (OTCPK:TCEHY) WeChat platform. Telephone & Data Systems (TDS, 4.4%, +13.7%) announced a CEO transition and reiterated that the pending sale of the firm's subsidiary's (US Cellular, 4.1%, +10.3%) wireless operations and select spectrum is expected to close in mid-2025.

TOP TEN SELECTED HOLDINGS*
T-Mobile US Inc. (TMUS) 9.4%
Meta Platforms Inc. (META) 6.5
Deutsche Telekom AG (OTCQX:DTEGY) 6.1
Alphabet Inc. (GOOG),(GOOGL) 5.7
Softbank Group Corp. (OTCPK:SFTBY) 5.5
Prosus NV (OTCPK:PROSY) 5.5
Telephone and Data Systems Inc. (TDS) 4.4
United States Cellular Corp. (USM) 4.1
Millicom International Cellular SA (TIGO) 3.6
Frontier Communications Parent Inc. (FYBR) 3.5
*Percent of net assets as of March 31, 2025.

The top detractor from Fund performance in 1Q was Alphabet (5.7%, -17.9%), as the company reported mixed 4Q'24 results and guided to meaningfully higher-than-anticipated CAPEX of $75 billion for fiscal 2025. In addition, investor concerns about potential economic slowdown and broader macroeconomic uncertainty put pressure on names with meaningful advertising exposure. SoftBank (5.5%, -14.2%) was down, as shares of its largest holding, Arm, declined by 13%, on a broad sell-off in Al names, as investors reassessed levels of differentiation and expected return on investment in the sector.

LET'S TALK STOCKS

Anterix (3.0% of net assets as of March 31, 2025) (ATEX - $36.60 - NASDAQ) is a wireless communications company focused on commercializing its spectrum assets to enable its targeted utility and critical infrastructure customers to deploy private broadband networks, technologies, and solutions. In February, ATEX announced that after receiving inbound interest in the company, it has engaged Morgan Stanley as its financial advisor to support a formal strategic review process.

Deutsche Telekom (6.1%) (DTE - $37.00 / €34.22 - Frankfurt) is the incumbent German telecom operator. Its U.S. business, T-Mobile US (TMUS), contributes over two-thirds of group consolidated EBITDA. In 2024, Deutsche Telekom shares generated a total return of 29% (in USD terms). While this result was largely attributable to a 40% gain in TMUS, the company's continued solid defense of the domestic German market, coupled with better-than-expected growth from European operations, are also credited. Marking the firm's TMUS stake to market, the remainder of DTE trades at a deeply discounted valuation of 2.5x 2025 estimated EBITDA.

Meta Platforms (6.5%) (ΜΕΤΑ – $576.36 - NASDAQ) is the leading global online social networking and social media service provider with nearly 3.4 billion daily active people across its 'family' of products. Meta generates nearly all of its revenue from advertising on its Family of Apps services, including Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, WhatsApp, etc. The firm is also making Al investments to supplement and enhance its services as well as developing its own large language model, LLaMA.

Millicom (3.6%) (TIGO - $30.27- NASDAQ) is a wireless carrier serving over 41 million mobile customers in Latin America. It also operates cable and fixed broadband businesses with approximately 9 million revenue-generating units (RGUs). In January 2025, the firm's board approved a new shareholder remuneration policy under which it proposed to resume regular cash dividends ($3.00/share annual dividend will be subject to AGM approval in May).

Prosus (5.5%) (PRX - $46.04 / €42.58 - Amsterdam) contains the non-South African Internet and digital assets of Naspers, with key listed assets including a 22.5% stake in Tencent and a 28% interest in Delivery Hero. After a brief M&A hiatus, the firm announced two acquisitions under CEO Fabricio Bloisi, who took over in July (Despegar, an online travel agency in Latin America, for $1.4 billion and Just Eat Takeaway.com for €4.1 billion). Simultaneously, Prosus continues the share repurchase (funded by gradual sale of its stake in Tencent) to reduce the discount to NAV.

COMPARATIVE RESULTS

Average Annual Returns through March 31, 2025 (A)

Gabelli Global Content & Connectivity Fund QTR 1 Year 5 Year 10 Year 15 Year Since Inception (11/01/93)
Class I (MUTF:GTTIX) (B) 3.70% 19.87% 11.74% 4.76% 5.72% 6.89%
MSCI AC World Communication Services Index (C) (2.44) 15.49 13.53 7.07 7.91 N/A
MSCI AC World Index (C) (1.22) 7.68 15.71 9.39 9.45 7.94

Total returns and average annual returns reflect changes in share price, reinvestment of distributions, and are net of expenses. Performance returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized.

(A) Other classes of shares are available, with different characteristics. For additional information please contact your financial advisor or call 800-GABELLI.

(B) Returns for Class I Shares would have been lower had Gabelli Funds, LLC, (the "Adviser") not reimbursed certain expenses. The Class AAA Share NAVs are used to calculate performance for the period prior to the issuance of Class I Shares on January 11, 2008. The actual performance of the Class I Shares would have been higher due to lower expenses related to this class of shares. The Fund imposes a 2% redemption fee on shares sold or exchanged within seven days of purchase.

(C) The MSCI AC World Communication Services Index is an unmanaged index that measures the performance of Communication Services from around the world, the inception date of the index is December 29, 2000. The MSCI AC World Index is an unmanaged market capitalization weighted index that is designed to measure the equity market performance of developed and emerging markets, since inception performance is as of October 31, 1993. Dividends are considered reinvested. You cannot invest directly in an index.

Investing in foreign securities involves risks not ordinarily associated with investments in domestic issues, including currency fluctuation, economic, and political risks.

Returns represent past performance and do not guarantee future results. Investment returns and the principal value of an investment will fluctuate. When shares are redeemed, they may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data presented. Visit Home for performance information as of the most recent month end.

Funds concentrating in specific sectors may experience greater fluctuations in value than funds that are more diversified. Investing in foreign securities involves risks not ordinarily associated with investments in domestic issues including currency fluctuations, economic and political risks. 28
Total Net Assets: $66.7 Million
NAV (Class I): $22.14
Turnover: (A) 11%
Inception Date: 11/01/93
Gross/Net Expense Ratio: (B) 1.65%/0.91%
(A) For the twelve months ended December 31, 2024. (B) As of the current prospectus dated April 29, 2024. Net expense ratio after reimbursement from the Adviser. Effective through April 30, 2025, unless terminated early by the Fund's Board of Directors.
SHARE CLASS SYMBOL
Class AAA: GABTX
Class A: MUTF:GTCAX
Class I: GTTIX

Sergey Dluzhevskiy, CFA

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Argan: Fundamental Momentum Continues, But Is It Sustainable?
data.description:
Thesis

I last wrote about Argan Inc. (NYSE:AGX) in early March. At that time, the company had already surged 120% over the last 12 months, and its price charts were also indicating some kind of weakness. This, combined with expensive valuation, led me to issue a neutral (hold rating) on the stock despite strong tailwinds that the company was witnessing. However, stock price performance post my neutral rating was quite remarkable, delivering ~82% in a matter of three months, leaving me in awe on the sidelines.

During these three months, AGX came out with two remarkable results, both beating the consensus estimates by a wide margin. Moreover, order book growth was also quite surprising.

AGX is going through quite strong fundamental momentum, but I think it is not sustainable, as competition would eventually rise and lead to margin and return deterioration. Moreover, perception would also play a big role in governing stock price, as we witnessed earlier this year when the launch of an efficient AI model (DEEPSEEK) resulted in a sharp price cut for AGX. However, I believe the price momentum could continue in the near future owing to strong fundamental momentum and economic reality would take time to materialize.

Therefore, I am reiterating my neutral (hold) rating on the stock.

Fundamental momentum continues

Earlier this week, AGX came out with splendid 1QFY26 results. The company reported a solid beat on the bottom-line front driven by exceptional margin expansion, while the top line came in line with estimates, albeit growing ~23% YoY. These extraordinary results came in after the even more extraordinary results in 4Q25.

While the top and bottom lines are good, a more forward-looking metric, the order book, was a lot better. The company reported a record order book of ~$1.9 billion, growing 130% YoY and 35% QoQ. The momentum in the order book is quite remarkable, which is expected to continue into the foreseeable future, as management has guided to add several more projects in the next few months, enabling the company to post a backlog "significantly over $2 billion by the end of the year." It is important to note that the current order book is already near $2 billion, so assuming a year-end order book over $2.5-$3 billion would not be too surprising, but management did not use this figure so as to keep market expectations in check, which are already running hot.

As I have discussed in my prior article, AGX is witnessing significant tailwinds from the power EPC sector owing to higher power demand thanks to increasing AI usage and EV-related tailwinds. Moreover, within the power sector, capex for renewable power generation, especially gas-based power plants, is increasing rapidly. AGX having its expertise in developing renewable and gas-powered power plants is helping the company to garner the most from this tailwind.

Moreover, given AGX's strong liquidity position and zero debt balance sheet, I don't believe it would be hesitant to add a few more large-scale projects going forward. Given the tailwinds and strong balance sheet position, I believe the fundamental momentum is quite intact and AGX should continue to surprise the street going forward. However, expectations are quite high now.

Valuation

As I have just mentioned, the expectations are quite high; it is quite visible in the valuation ratios. As per Seeking Alpha's Quant rating system, AGX has an F grade for valuation. As Seeking alpha's Quant uses a range of valuation metrics to arrive at this grade, it would be a fair assumption to believe AGX's valuations aren't cheap at all.

However, let's use Non GAAP PE to gain a better insight into the company's valuation. The current non-GAAP forward PE ratio stands at ~37.9x compared to the ~20x sector median and 24x 5-year average. But let's be honest here, the sector comprises a lot of companies that aren't benefiting from the tailwinds that AGX is benefiting from, and the current tailwinds for AGX are not comparable to those of 5 years earlier.

I believe AGX is at a juncture where growth in demand and supply would have a lot of influence over the stock price of AGX. While it is true for most of the stocks, it is even more so for AGX, as valuation comfort is clearly not present and growth is what justifies current valuation alone. So delving deeper into valuation ratios would be a futile exercise. Rather, gauging the sustainability of current fundamentals would be the right question to ask.

Are good days for AGX sustainable

The main question is: Is the growth AGX is witnessing sustainable? If yes, how long will it continue? Well, the short answer is no. For how long it will sustain, it would depend on the pace of demand growth as well as the pace of supply growth.

It is well known, AGX does not perform any differentiated business; it's just an EPC company with expertise in developing power plants, especially renewable and gas-based. Although one should need expertise and reputation to bag orders, it's no rocket science to replicate this business model. What I am trying to say is that the AGX business does not have any kind of strong moat and there are existing players that have similar capabilities, which can drive up competition. Given AGX's supernormal return on equity of ~27%, competition is bound to increase going forward.

We are in a phase where demand has shocked the market and supply is catching up. Once demand dries out or supply picks up pace, higher margins/return on equity are bound to fall. In that case, current elevated valuation ratios would look even more expensive. However, given the fundamental momentum, I don't think we are in a situation where margins/ROE would fall dramatically in the near future. Till then, the stock price should continue to surprise people on the sidelines, creating FOMO. However, I believe one should not fall for this, given the risks. Moreover, public perception could also trigger a major sell-off in these stocks. For instance, public perception about demand can also lead to large drawdowns in stock prices, as we have seen in early January when the stock price fell considerably due to the launch of a more efficient AI model that requires less energy.

Given the lack of valuation safety, the lack of an economic moat to sustain this extraordinary growth and the risk of a large drawdown due to changing market perception, I would like to stay on the sidelines despite such strong fundamental tailwinds for AGX.

data.title: After Q1 2026, My Planet Labs Thesis Is Still Intact
data.description:
Thesis

Planet Labs (NYSE:PL), a San Francisco outfit started by ex-NASA engineers, runs one of the biggest fleets of Earth-watching satellites around. It churns out constant images of the planet to feed data-hungry clients in defense, farming, climate, and whatever else needs eyes in the sky, pitching itself as the go-to infrastructure layer for public and private decision-makers. After its Q1 2026 earnings release, I argue that what we’ve got here is a company inching from promise to prove, catching big-picture trends in defense, climate, and AI-fed analytics. It’s not profitable yet, not really close, but the contracts, tech rollouts, and government buys show a firm working its way into the wiring of the new data economy, one satellite feed at a time.

Inside Planet Labs' Q1 2026 Performance

Back in December, I began my research on Planet. Impressed by what looked like a solid company in a frothy tech landscape, I went ahead and initiated my coverage with a "Buy" rating.

Planet had been compounding revenue at 25% annually since 2020, and unlike so many other small-cap tech plays, it was growing smart. With gross margins of 52.79%, well above the sector median of 31.94%, and long-term debt making up just 3.06% of its capital structure, Planet stood out to me back then, while most of the hype stocks in that risk-on stampede were all inflated promises and bleeding balance sheets. Case in point, gross profit had flipped from a negative $6.7 million in 2020 to more than $124 million in 2024.

Fast-forward, and judging by today's +50% move post Q1 2026 earnings, and the thesis has only matured. The commercial satellite imaging market that Planet serves is entering what looks like a structurally expansive era. Future Market Insights projects that the total addressable market will climb from $5.87 billion in 2025 to around $15.33 billion by 2035, growing at a 12.3% CAGR. That’s a real market expansion taking shape, and more to the point, Planet isn't literally helping draw the map.

Take the Defense & Intelligence segment, for instance. That market alone is forecast to grow at 11.7% annually, pushed by rising geopolitical instability and a steady march of increased defense budgets worldwide. Agencies like the U.S. Department of Defense and the National Reconnaissance Office are embedding real-time, commercial satellite feeds into mission-critical systems. Planet’s long-term contracts and relationships, like the recent multi-year, eight-figure annual contract from a European defense agency, show it’s becoming part of the decision-making spine for sovereign nations.

But defense is only a section of the landscape. The Agriculture and Forestry vertical is poised to grab 17% of market share this year. How come? From crop yield optimization to soil moisture tracking, satellite data is becoming the invisible layer behind smart farming. Global initiatives like the USDA’s $500 million precision ag program (made available by Biden's admin) and Europe’s Copernicus program are demanding Earth observation (EO) insights, and Planet is already delivering. In fact, Planet’s environmental monitoring role is expanding so much that it’s now the primary subcontractor on a $95 million contract awarded by the California Air Resource Board through the Carbon Mapper initiative.

Then there’s the carbon credit economy. Planet’s work with Laconic and its embedded role in Carbon Mapper aren't laying a foundation for Planet to become a key verifier in the carbon offset market, one expected to quadruple in value from around $114.8 billion in 2024 to $474.2 billion by 2034. But the space is still plagued by greenwashing, lack of standardization, and regulatory fragmentation. Planet’s edge here will depend on whether it can deliver trusted, auditable data at scale, and if it does, it's in line to become the de facto source of truth in a trillion-dollar environmental ledger.

But, of course, all this would still be speculative without hard evidence that the business is executing. Enter Planet’s most recent quarter: revenue came in at $66.3 million, beating expectations by $4.01 million and marking 9.64% year-over-year growth. For the second straight quarter, it posted adjusted EBITDA profitability, $1.2 million this time, and achieved a major corporate milestone: positive free cash flow. It generated $17.3 million in free cash and ended the quarter with $8 million in the bank.

Gross margins expanded again, from 55% to 59% year-over-year, reinforcing that the subscription-heavy business model is scaling with 97% of the company's Annual Contract Value (ACV) being subscription-based, which gives revenue that sticky, recurring flavor I love to see in the stocks I own. Even better, the company’s net dollar retention rate grew 103%, improving to 104% when including customer win backs, meaning not only are clients staying, they’re spending more overtime.

That strength is showing up in sector performance too, with the Defense & Intelligence revenue growing over 20% year-over-year, carried by those aforementioned contracts. Meanwhile, Planet landed a seven-figure expansion deal with an existing government client to scale its capabilities from land monitoring to maritime surveillance. Civil government growth is catching up, too. Beyond Carbon Mapper, Planet expanded partnerships with both Germany’s Federal Agency for Cartography and Geodesy (BKG) and the Welsh government.

On the tech side, the company now has two of its next-gen satellites, Tanager-1 and Pelican-2 operational in orbit, giving it more than two and a half years of on-orbit experience with its advanced smallsat platform. And the company’s Aircraft Detection Analytic Feed delivers near-real-time intelligence on flight activity, tapping into high-frequency data processing and AI. It’s also partnering with Anthropic to feed geospatial data into large language models, pointing to future ambitions around automated analytics and possibly AI-native interfaces for planetary data.

Finally, internationally, Planet’s revenue from EMEA and Asia Pacific grew over 30% year-over-year, and it just inked a $230 million satellite-build contract in Asia. That translates to a foothold in fast-growing markets like India and China, where EO growth is tracking 17.2% and 15.8% CAGR, respectively. The best part? Backlog. The company now sits on $527 million in total backlog, up 140% year-over-year, with $451.9 million in remaining performance obligations, 45% of which is expected to convert within the next 12 months.

Breaking Down Planet Labs’ Valuation, Growth Drivers & Headwinds

Moving on to the valuation, the current metrics start to bend convention and seem to dislocate from it. Planet’s enterprise value to sales multiple comes in at 3.95x on a trailing basis and 3.56x forward. That represents an 85% to 96% premium over the median for the Industrials sector, multiples that are typically reserved for software companies with gross margins north of 80%. But, highlights aside, Planet, by contrast, is a satellite imaging firm that has yet to produce positive EBITDA, let alone net income.

Next up, the Price/Sales ratio: 4.59x trailing and 4.30x forward. These are nearly triple the sector average. Again, this might be justified if Planet were showing scalable revenue growth with consistent margin expansion, but the underlying cash flow story isn't showing that to us in the data.

The forward Price/Cash Flow ratio is at 49.69x, versus a sector median of 15.27. In other words, investors are being asked to pay nearly 50 times for every projected future dollar of cash.

And so, what's underpinning today's price pop and this towering multiple structure? Optimistic EPS growth estimates, it seems. Analysts expect earnings per share to rise by 24.38% in January 2026, 26.49% in 2027, and then grow to 92.30% in 2028. These might make sense for a fast-scaling SaaS platform with a clean margin profile and product-market fit. But Planet Labs isn’t that.

It’s a company that, as of now, has negative EBIT, EBITDA, and net income margins. There is no historical profitability here either, no proven base from which these sharp EPS curves could spring. And while long-term EPS CAGR consensus is around a more restrained 11.21% for the sector, even that feels tenuous when held against the sheer weight of its cash flow losses and capital intensity.

Going by Seeking Alpha's current metrics, cash flow looks like where the valuation optimism really begins to unravel. Planet’s trailing twelve-month operating cash flow is a negative $14.37 million. And the levered free cash flow margin gives us the same idea: -11.95%. In practical terms, that means that the company is burning cash at multiple layers of the income and capital stack, with little sign of conversion from revenue to usable cash. Capital expenditures, which now consume 18.13% of sales, six times the sector average, are rising fast too.

These spending patterns are made all the more precarious by the company’s profit profile. Gross margins are definitely solid at 57.65%, but they are systematically consumed by costs further down the P&L. The EBITDA margin is at -24.38%, EBIT at -42.03%, and net income margin at -50.42%. While gross margin can be an indicator of value-add, some might see that as meaningless when downstream margins look like a bleeding edge.

The latest quarter brought in some top-line growth, but the cracks are widening. Civil government revenue dropped year-over-year, largely due to the expiration of the NICFI contract with Norway. That contract was once a cornerstone of Planet’s pitch to public sector clients; now its loss underscores the fragility of public funding. Simultaneously, North American revenue declined, with management blaming contract adjustments and persistent underperformance in the agriculture vertical.

Customer count, meanwhile, slipped to 919, a sequential decline and this might seem small and watered down in scope by management who attributed it to a deliberate shift away from smaller clients in favor of enterprise-scale accounts. I think that that could bear fruit in the long term, boosting average revenue per customer. But in the near future, depending how much risk is on for growth companies like Planet, that could become hard to convince investors that things are going well when the number of customers is going down.

That brings me to guidance. For Q2, Planet projects revenue of $65 to $67 million, essentially flat compared to Q1. There's also the reversal in profitability: adjusted EBITDA is forecast to fall back into negative territory, between -$2 million and -$4 million. So this follows a brief, tenuous flirtation with adjusted profitability, now undone. For the full fiscal year, adjusted EBITDA losses are expected to range from $12 million to $7 million. And CapEx is forecast to come up again in Q2, projected at $17 million to $22 million, due to delayed satellite procurement and launch activities from the prior quarter. Annual CapEx remains unchanged, in the high range of $50 million to $65 million, emphasizing a continued capital-intensive path.

Even margin guidance looks to be tilting downward. Management expects Q2 gross margins to decline to 56%–57%, for perspective, that's down from Q1’s 59%, primarily due to added costs from the JSAT satellite services contract and an expansion in partner solutions. But the leadership insists that long-term margins will normalize.

Meanwhile, Planet faces looming risk from its largest and most sensitive customer base: the U.S. government. Proposed reductions in federal spending only layer on more geopolitical volatility that's difficult to price. A 25% cut to NASA’s FY26 science budget has already been floated, and whispers of a 30% reduction in the EOCL program have surfaced. Both of those programs are integral to Planet’s pipeline. And although the company positions itself as a budget-friendly solution in an age of tightening federal belts, I'd say that this claim remains untested, considering that many of its contracts are subject to continuing resolutions or early termination clauses, rendering the forecast horizon even murkier. Finally, the commercial side offers little relief. Revenues from commercial clients were flat year-over-year. Despite strategic reorientation toward higher-fit verticals, there’s little sign of meaningful acceleration.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, I’m sticking with a Buy rating. The valuation’s rich and the bottom line’s still a mess, but revenue’s climbing, margins are widening, and the subscription model’s holding up. What I like seeing is that, ultimately, they’re landing real contracts, in defense, ag, and climate, and the satellite imaging market’s not shrinking anytime soon. It’s not a Strong Buy right now, but the fundamentals back up the bullish case.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Gabelli International Small Cap Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
INVESTMENT SCORECARD

International equities usefully outperformed U.S. equities during the quarter. For the first three months of the year, the EAFE Index, which measures the developed equity markets outside North America, appreciated by 3.8%. Overseas returns were helped by the weakness in the dollar (DXY), which declined relative to both the euro and the yen. Among the larger overseas markets, Germany rose by 15.3%, the UK by 8.5%, and Japan fell by 0.6%. For comparison, the S&P lost 4.3% and the technology heavy NASDAQ Index dropped by 10.3%. In the two largest overseas markets, small capitalization stocks outperformed larger stocks in Japan but underperformed in the UK.

Aside from the new U.S. administration's economic and other pronouncements, two events during the quarter stand out in terms of influencing capital markets. First, DEEPSEEK, a Chinese based entity, surprisingly launched an Al model at a far lower cost than many thought possible. This soured sentiment among investors in some of the large U.S. based technology companies that had performed so strongly over the past few years. Time will tell whether this is a significant development. Second, in response to concerns about security, European countries have decided to spend much more on defense. Germany, whose debt levels are low, actually changed its constitution to allow for debt constraints to be lifted. This will pave the way for huge infrastructure projects and higher defense spending. In response, the German equity markets rallied strongly, while bond yields and the euro rose as investors anticipate higher growth.

The new U.S. administration has set its sights on lowering the U.S. trade deficit. This has created some economic uncertainty, which could lead to slower global growth in the short term. Generally, slower growth is negative for small capitalization stocks but contrary to this many small capitalization companies tend to be more domestically focused and thus less exposed to reduced levels of trade. In Europe, the central bank continues to lower interest rates as inflation declines and growth remains sluggish. However, fiscal stimulus is generally positive for corporate profits. So, domestically oriented companies in Europe should benefit, assuming Germany follows through with its recently announced spending plans. The outlook is very uncertain, as established economic and security structures are being examined and challenged by the U.S. Administration and, of course, for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction.

TOP TEN SELECTED HOLDINGS*
Siegfried Holding AG (OTCPK:SGFEF) 3.8%
Endeavour Mining Plc (OTCQX:EDVMF) 3.8
Alamos Gold Inc. (AGI) 3.6
Chemring Group plc. (OTCPK:CMGMF) 3.6
Genius Sports Ltd (GENI) 3.3
Loomis AB (OTCPK:LOIMF) 3.0
Perseus Mining Ltd. (OTCPK:PMNXF) 2.9
GMO Internet Inc. (OTCPK:GMOYF) 2.9
Westgold Resources Ltd. (OTCQX:WGXRF) 2.7
Laurent-Perrier (OTC:LPRRF) 2.7

*Percent of net assets as of March 31, 2025.

Some of our gold mining stocks performed strongly on the back of a rising gold price. For example, Alamos Gold (3.6% of net assets as of March 31, 2025) and Endeavour Mining (3.8%) rallied by 45.0% and 36.1%, respectively. However, our top performer was IVECO Group (OTCPK:IVCGF, 2.4%), an Italian based truck manufacturer that rose by 68.3% as investors were attracted to its military vehicle division. Otherwise, Dragerwerk (OTCPK:DGWPF, 2.3%, +42.5%) and Loomis (3.0%, +32.9%) were standout performers. Small capitalization stocks tend to be volatile, and we had a number of losers, especially in the UK market, where Manchester United (MANU, 1.0%), Treatt (OTCPK:TTTRF, 1.2%), and Tristel (OTC:TSNLF, 1.1%) all declined by over 20%. During the three months under review, we initiated three new positions: Bridgepoint Group (0.9%), Kawasaki Heavy Industries (OTCPK:KWHIY, 1.2%), and TOWA Corporation (OTCPK:TOWCF, 0.9%). These purchases were funded by the proceeds from the sale of three Japanese holdings: Aida Engineering (OTCPK:ADERY), NCC Group (OTCPK:NCCGF), and Zojirushi (OTC:ZOJIF). Otherwise, we reduced our holdings in a number of companies that generally had recently performed strongly.

LET'S TALK STOCKS

Genius Sports Ltd (3.3% of net assets as of March 31, 2025) (GENI - £10.01/$10.01 - NYSE), headquartered in London, England, provides data services and technology to the sports, sports betting, and media industries. It delivers live data, oddsmaking, risk management, and player marketing tools to nearly all regulated sportsbooks. Genius partners with broadcasters to offer alternative feeds that enhance live footage, using optical tracking and real-time overlays featuring stats and betting odds. It also supports leagues with global bet monitoring, integrity audits, investigations, and education to protect against match-fixing. It is the exclusive global distributor of official NFL data to the regulated sports betting market. With a scalable tech and data platform, Genius expects operating leverage to drive significant margin expansion and free cash flow as sports betting demand continues to grow.

IVECO Group (2.4%) (IVG - $16.44 - OTC), headquartered in Turin, Italy, was spun out of CNHI in Jan 2022. IVECO is a leading European Truck and Bus OEM, with a product portfolio covering Heavy Duty, Medium-Duty, and LCVs. They are the leaders in LCV cab-chassis in Europe as well as Intercity Buses. IVECO also are a leading Powertrain manufacturer with a top 3 engine manufacturer position globally and 60% of the output for 3rd parties. The company also operates in Defence trucks via JVs with Rheinmetall, BAE Systems, and Horiba-Mira for tanks, amphibious, and uncrewed ground vehicles. With the launch of MY24, the company has a refreshed portfolio and is considering strategic alternatives for the Defence segment, which has seen interest from several suitors per press reports.

data.title: Sprinklr: Good Developments, Not Good Enough
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Sprinklr (NYSE:CXM) previously with a hold rating, as I did not expect valuation upside until CXM is able to deliver tangible results from the initiatives implemented by the new CEO. I remain hold rated. While there are some early signs of stabilization and better execution, the revenue growth and net retention trends still suggest that a full turnaround is going to take time.

1Q26 earnings show some signs of stability.

CXM's 1Q26 results showed some really good signs of a turnaround. As a recap, here are some key numbers from the results. Total revenue came in at $205.5 million, up 5% y/y, with subscription revenue growing 4% y/y to $184.1 million. While headline growth is still down a lot from the 13% since 1Q25, 1Q26 did mark the first quarter in a long while that y/y growth accelerated sequentially. Profitability is another area of highlight. Adj. operating income hit $36.7 million, which is an 18% margin, up sharply from 11% in 1Q25 and ~200 bps above guidance. All of this directly translated to solid free cash flow [FCF]. CXM delivered a record $80.7 million FCF, and that would have been closer to $92.5 million if not for restructuring-related cash outflows. Overall, this was a very decent and good print that shows CXM is executing well.

Transformation Efforts Show Early Promise

When I last looked at CXM, many of their transformation efforts were still at the planning stage. But based on the 1Q26 update, it feels like they're beginning to gain some traction. The CEO clearly sounded more confident on the 1Q26 call, pointing to progress across several key initiatives. One of the initiatives that has shown early promise is Project Bearhug. This initiative aims to deepen CXM's relationships with its top 500 customers (who contribute ~80% of revenue). So far, CXM has spoken to hundreds of these clients (about 200 as of the end of May), and notably, these efforts have translated into expanded use cases. This is very helpful to know because it shows that there are a bunch of existing clients that are still underserved, and all CXM needs to do is to approach them. If these efforts continue to translate into upsells and stickier relationships, it would really improve the growth momentum and ease churn.

We're encouraged by the early results and new expanded use cases as we help companies drive accelerated ROI across the Sprinklr platform. Company 1Q26 earnings

The other update I had that got me more positive on the transformation plan is regarding the SKU front. This area has also clearly progressed past the initial planning stage. In the call, management mentioned that the product pipeline is the healthiest it's been in 18 months across Social, Marketing, and Insights (core portfolio). Clearly, CXM has shifted its resources to focus on its core business, and this is great because this is where CXM thrives. Sales incentives have been realigned to support core growth, and there's a new initiative called "Project Tiger Shark" focused on accelerating product innovation across the core SKUs. It would be very interesting to see what the new products are that CXM is going to launch, but this shows that management is making a serious effort to double down on what works.

One area that I would particularly like to see more development is the AI side of things. CXM has something good going on here. Its new AI-native platform could become a meaningful growth driver, especially in service. So far, this AI platform is helping customers reach agent deflection rates between 30% and 80%, which is massive since implies the average employee productivity is now up by 1.6x (average 40% deflection rate). If CXM can push out more of such AI products that generate value for clients, it would go a long way to help accelerate revenue growth.

Lastly, I think a very important proof point that shows CXM is on the right path to turnaround is that it continues to land large enterprise wins. In 1Q26, they had 146 customers generating >$1 million in subscription revenue, up 6% y/y. Although this is not an explosive growth, it supports the idea that the platform still resonates with global brands. Some of the major brands highlighted include companies like Calvin Klein, LG Electronics, and Pepsi. More impressively, CXM closed a major deal with a Fortune 500 medical tech firm to consolidate global operations and another with a large UK retailer to revamp its contact center. These are not small deployments, so they do speak very well of CXM's ability to support complex use cases at scale.

Persistent Headwinds and Still Uncertain Turnaround Timeline

Despite the encouraging improvements, I am still concerned about the near-term growth outlook, and this is the same caution I raised in my prior note. Putting all the developments aside, in 1Q26, revenue grew just 5% y/y, with subscription revenue up 4% y/y. It is still very far from the past years' double-digit revenue growth. It also doesn't help to learn that management's FY26 guide calls for just 3% y/y subscription growth at the midpoint, and they even went one step ahead to say that any meaningful re-acceleration likely won't happen until late FY26 or FY27.

Retention and expansion are also still areas of concern. Net dollar retention [NDR] continues to erode-down to 102% for 1Q26, from 107% just two quarters ago. The implication here is that CXM needs to rely more on winning new customers to grow, and that is not going to be easy if NDR continues to decline. A lot of work needs to be done on this front. If revenue grows and NDR contracts, I don't think the market will view it favorably. Even though the number of >$1 million annual recurring revenue [ARR] customers grew 6% y/y, 1Q26 still saw a decline of two of such customers sequentially. This isn't a disaster, but when I add this to the list of concerns I have, it just makes it harder to turn bullish.

Valuation

Given the way things are, I don't think a near-term growth acceleration is likely, which means CXM is still very far away from achieving double-digit revenue growth like what peers are delivering - HubSpot (HUBS), Freshworks (FRSH), and Braze (BRZE). I remained with the view that so long as CXM growth doesn't accelerate, it will continue to trade at a discount to peers. How much discount is debatable, but given the stark difference in growth outlook-peers with ~16% average growth and CXM with low single digits (almost 4x delta)-50% difference in multiples is not aggressive.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating for CXM. It is indeed making progress, with promising traction from key transformation initiatives. But growth remains slow, NDR is trending down, and the overall timeline for reacceleration stretches well into FY27. With revenue growth expected to remain at low single digits, I don't see a clear catalyst for multiple expansion in the near term.

data.title: The Gabelli Global Mini Mites Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
INVESTMENT SCORECARD

During the first quarter, the Gabelli Global Mini Mites Fund declined 6.05%, compared to a return of (3.91)% for the S&P Developed Small Cap Index.

The top contributors to performance in the quarter were Velan Inc. (VLNSF, 2.5% of net assets as of March 31, 2025) and Avio S.p.A. (1.9%) Velan Inc. is a manufacturer of highly engineered valves and was up 62% during 1Q25, following the announcement of the sale of its French subsidiary to Framatome for $208 million and the concurrent divestiture of its asbestos liabilities to Global Risk Capital. The French transaction closed on March 31, and we believe it paves the way for an outright sale of the remaining assets. Avio S.p.A., listed in Milan, Italy, is a manufacturer of space transport and tactical missiles propulsion systems. It gained 29% in 1Q25 on the heels of solid 2024 results and positive long-term outlook. The company recently signed €350 million in new contracts with the European Space Agency and €150 million with MBDA Italy for the supply of rocket motors for CAMM-ER missiles. The EU is targeting a common fund of €800 billion to rearm Europe, and Avio is set to benefit from it. The company is also setting up a U.S. subsidiary to explore opportunities in the U.S. Avio recently signed research and development contracts with Raytheon and with the U.S. Army Combat Capabilities Development Command Aviation & Missile Center. If successful, these partnerships will prove to be very lucrative for Avio.

The top detractors in the quarter were Applied Optoelectronics, Inc. (AAOI, 0.2%) and Harvard Bioscience, Inc. (HBIO, 0.3%). Applied Optoelectronics is an integrated provider of fiber optic networking products. The company is pursuing multi-year DOCSIS 4.0 network upgrades in cable television infrastructure and 400G data center products. It formed a strategic partnership with Microsoft for the development of 400G products, including exposure to artificial intelligence infrastructure. It is planning to make a large capex investment of $150 million in 2025 to build manufacturing capacity. It reported December quarter sales in line with its guidance and its shares declined 58% on the back of a sell off in data center/Al exposed stocks. Nvidia's plans to scale Al architecture using CPO (co-packaged optics) may present long-term revenue headwinds as the industry transitions from transceivers to CPOs. The company issued 7.9 million warrant shares to Amazon (AMZN) in March 2025, of which 1.3 million vested immediately on issuance, and with the remainder exercisable at $23.69. Harvard Bioscience manufactures life science equipment to support research and drug discovery. Their products include spectrophotometers, amino acid analyzers, mesh multielectrode arrays (MEA), clamp amplifiers, implantable telemetry products, and mobile activity rack systems. Shares declined 73% during 1Q25 as the company raised going concern risks as it seeks to explore alternative sources of capital to refinance outstanding debt due by 30 June 2025.

TOP TEN SELECTED HOLDINGS*
Tredegar Corp. (TG) 7.1%
Intevac Inc. 3.2
Velan Inc. (OTCPK:VLNSF) 2.5
Ampco-Pittsburgh Corp. (AP) 2.1
Park-Ohio Holdings Corp. (PKOH) 2.0
LB Foster Co. (FSTR) 1.9
Myers Industries Inc. (MYE) 1.9
Avio S.p.A. 1.9
Village Super Market Inc. (VLGEA) 1.8
Clarus Corp. (CLAR) 1.8
*Percent of net assets as of March 31, 2025

LET'S TALK STOCKS

Gencor Industries Inc. (0.7% of net assets as of March 31, 2025) (GENC - $12.16 - NYSE ) designs, builds and sells equipment and materials used for the production of asphalt and highway construction machines. Shares appreciated during the quarter as its fiscal second quarter results outpaced expectations; Gencor reported over 30% sales and earnings growth. Additionally, its backlog rose to over $50 million, a favorable indicator as it benefits from large scale highway and infrastructure projects associated with the 2021 Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act.

Lee Enterprises Inc. (1.5%) (LEE - $10.38 - NASDAQ) is a legacy newspaper business serving 72 mid-sized markets based in Davenport, lowa, that is transforming itself into a digital news and subscription business. Lee's shares initially began appreciating last September following the sale of publishing assets to the Hoffman Media Group and subsequent filing that the Hoffmann Trust initiated a 5% ownership position, which expanded to nearly 10% by mid-December 2024. More recently, on March 20th, David Hoffmann sent a letter to the company in an effort to open discussions regarding a potential acquisition of LEE (but it did not contain any information around a potential acquisition price/terms), and the company responded on March 24th with an offering to enter into a confidentiality agreement, which would allow Hoffman to make a more formal proposal including a price and ability to finance the transaction. Two days later, LEE's Board of Directors unanimously approved an extension of the company's shareholder rights plan for another year (until March 27, 2026). On the company's Q1 earnings call, management reiterated its 2025 outlook for digital growth of 7%-10% and low single digit EBITDA growth, supported by $40mm of annualized cost savings LEE expects to have executed by Q2.

STRATTEC Security Corp. (1.3%) (STRT - $39.46 - NASDAQ) designs and manufacturers access systems and security solutions such as key fobs and power lift gate systems for vehicles. Shares appreciated following the release of its June results, which reflected growth across new products and pricing that contributed to higher margins. In June, STRATTEC named a new CEO, Jennifer Slater, who is undergoing a comprehensive review of the business, which we expect to conclude with a new operating strategy and financial targets. To date, Ms. Slater has identified several areas for operational efficiency improvements that should help drive greater profitability and gross margin predictability for the company.

COMPARATIVE RESULTS: Average Annual Returns through March 31, 2025 (A)

data.title: JAKKS Pacific: A Rare Positive Risk Premium
data.description:
Those who know me best know that I have a few obsessions. I like playing Dungeons & Dragons. I like painting. I like writing. I like the soap opera “The Young & The Restless.” All of these obsessions pale in comparison to my strong desire to make the lives of my readers as pleasant as possible. One of the ways I do this is by offering up a thesis statement at the beginning of each article. This benefits those readers who want a bit more than what they get from a title and bullet points, and less than they’d get from the full “Doyle article experience.” So, I’m going to be taking a relatively small position in this JAKKS Pacific (NASDAQ:JAKK) stock this afternoon (June 5th) for a few reasons. First, I like the financial performance, and the strength of the balance sheet. Although the dividend has been reintroduced only recently, I think it’s sustainable. Finally, the stock is attractively priced relative to the risk-free alternative, which makes it a bit of a standout at the moment. Thus ends the thesis statement portion of the article. If you read on, that’s on you.

Introduction of The Business

It's been written that the purpose of these articles on Seeking Alpha is to give investors ideas that they can then explore in greater depth, putting each investment idea in the context of their own circumstances. This is why I tend to focus on analysis that may not be too obvious. That written, I also want to let you know about the business in case you're unable to access the company's investor page, or latest slide deck. That's just one more of the value added services I want to offer my readers. You're welcome.

JAKKS is a leading designer, manufacturer, and marketer of toys and consumer products sold throughout the world. The company's seen very strong growth recently from Sonic the Hedgehog 3, Disney Moana 2, and DreamWorks Animation Dog Man among others.

One of the reasons the first quarter of this year was much better (less bad) than previous is because the company launched a host of new products that seem to be doing well. In particular, I'm impressed by the fact that the company's Dolls, Role Play/Dress Up business increased sales 37% from the year ago period. I also like the geographic diversification, as evidenced by the fact that North American sales were higher by 25%, and International sales were up 29% from the year ago period.

The company sells a wide range of entertainment-inspired products featuring premier licensed properties, including The Simpsons, Disney's Little Mermaid, and The Super Mario Brothers Movie properties. The following presents a more full view of the properties.

I hope this short introduction to the business doesn't interfere with my desire to save as much of your time as possible. I guess you'll be reading about the business eventually anyway, so it may as well be here.

Financial Snapshot

I think the financial history is reasonable enough. The company is subject to the business cycle, obviously, but it seems to be on an uptick. The reason I suggest this is the fact that revenue in the first quarter of 2025 was up by about 25.75%, and net loss has shrunk by about 83.6% from a loss of $14.5 million last year to $2.4 million in 2025. Additionally, the capital structure is reasonably strong, with total assets of $406 million to total liabilities of $170.8 million. Additionally, I think it’s relevant that $59 million of the $406 is cash, so there’s no problem with liquidity here in my view. Given the above, I’d be happy to buy the shares if the valuation is reasonable and if the dividend is sustainable.

Dividend Sustainability

As I just suggested above, the sustainability of the dividend is of critical importance here, and I want to focus on it because it’s got such a short pedigree in this case. At least one investor commented on my Mattel post that they prefer JAKK to Mattel because of the dividend. Thus, in the absence of the dividend, it’s reasonable to suppose that some investors would abandon the name, which would have obvious implications on price.

Although I love accrual accounting as much as it’s possible to love accrual accounting, I am of the view that when it comes to dividends and their sustainability, cash is king. So, I’m going to review JAKKS Pacific’s likely sources, and uses of cash. I’m in the mood to start by looking at sources, so I’m going to start looking at sources of cash. As of the latest 10-Q, the company has about $59.18 million in cash and equivalents on the balance sheet. Additionally, over the past three years, they’ve generated an average of $63.8 million in cash from operations. For those of you who may not have been victimized by a course or two in financial statement analysis, I’ll let you know that accounting standards “add back” to cash from operations the depreciation expense. While this may be technically correct when we’re looking only at cash, I have a problem with it because depreciation is a real thing. It imposes a real economic cost, so I’m also going to calculate cash from operations, but with the depreciation add back stripped out. When I do this, my adjusted average cash from operations figure drops to $54 million.

At the same time, the company has invested an average of about $10.7 million in the business over the past three years. So far, I’m pretty optimistic.

In terms of obligations, I’ve plucked the following from page 34 of the latest 10-K for your enjoyment and edification. In terms of financial obligations, it seems that 2025 is a pretty "spicy meatball", as the young people say. The company is on the hook for $72.2 million this year, and $39 million next year, and $18 million in 2027. Please note, per the highlighted portion below, the one variable here is taxes. We need to always remember that all governments everywhere need to wet their beak. I mean, they need a taste because this business is done in their territory. It’s a matter of respect.

In spite of the risk of government representatives showing up in shiny suits , making offers that can't be refused, and talking about what a nice business JAKK Pacific is, and how much of a shame it’d be if something happened to it, I think the dividend is sustainable. If the valuation is reasonable, I’d be happy to buy at current prices.

The Stock v Treasury Note

Some people would fear coming off as repetitive. If you read my stuff regularly, you know that this is not a fear that I suffer from. Given that, I’m going to write it one more time. In the world of investing, everything’s relative. Also, we investors are always looking for the greatest risk-adjusted returns. We're not looking for "returns." We're looking for "risk-adjusted returns." One of the implications of this point is that a stock, which is generally riskier than the 10 Year Treasury Note, must offer a better return than debt backed by the full faith and credit of Uncle Sam. Put another way, if a stock can’t generate the same level of cash flow as the risk-free rate, why on Earth would you buy it?

In terms of the specifics of comparing a given stock to the Treasury Note, I ask, and then answer, the question “at what rate will the dividend need to grow/can the dividend fall over the next decade for stock investors to receive the same cash flows as Treasury Note holders?” Please note that I think they should receive more than Treasury holders, given the risks involved, but baby steps. At the moment, the yield on the 10-Year Treasury Note is about 4.392%, and the dividend yield on JAKK is about 4.67%. Per the table below, the dividend could fall at a CAGR of about 1.5% for the shareholder to receive the same cash flows as the Treasury Note holder over the next decade. In my view, the dividend is more likely to rise, so the risk premium is actually positive here. This fact makes JAKKS a relatively rare stock at the moment.

Given the above, I'll be taking a small (300 shares) speculative position in this stock, and will likely add as the year progresses assuming the bull thesis remains intact.

Risks To My Thesis

Painful experience has shown me that my certainty is sometimes not warranted. It's for that reason that I want to write about the risks associated with this strategy. A superficial analysis might suggest that companies like this might be harmed by a recession. It turns out, though, that toys are relatively recession-resistant. Given that, I think the biggest risk to this thesis is an uptick in interest rates from current levels. One of the reasons I think this investment is quite attractive is because it offers a sustainable yield that's superior to the 10-Year Treasury Note. If the risk-free rate exceeds the yield here, the bullish thesis is materially muted in my view.

Conclusion

To sum up, I'm of the view that this company is growing its cash flows at a decent clip, and is therefore financially strong, particularly the capital structure. The dividend is sustainable in my view, given the delta between cash from operations, and cash needed for investment and contractual obligations. That dividend offers a higher yield at the moment than the risk-free rate, which makes this a relatively rare investment indeed at the moment. My advice to investors is to buy a small position here, and add to that position as the bullish thesis is borne out. I would also pay attention to the term structure of interest rates. If rates start to rise materially, the relative value of this investment will fall in my view, and that would call for obvious action.
